speaker: interviewer
text: We're interviewing Ernest Rubin. We're in Brooklyn, New York. And the interviewer is Rickey Halperin It's March 19, 1995. And the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, please, again the name of the ghetto that you were taken into.

speaker: interviewee
text: Satoraljaujhely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from Somotor?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 Kilometers exactly. This is exactly about seven miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken from Somotor to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Somotor, we walked. Everybody walked. My mother-- let her rest in peace-- they put her on the horse and a carriage that our neighbors, the Speigel family-- they had a big farm. They were in the lumber business. They were quite wealthy. They had horses. And the [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, the one who drives, who was doing the work-- he was driving in the-- Speigel, elderly ladies. They didn't let the young ones sit on the carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother-- because she was very sick already, my mother was in sanatorium. I don't know, if you-- in English, it's sanatorium. Because she was eating herself up, already, what happened, you know? So she gets sick and diabetic and all kinds of-- so she was in a sanatorium. If I would tell you who owned this, who did it-- you remember, Zukor, Adolf, from Hollywood? He had his family about 10 miles from Somotor. And he built this hospital. And we managed to take my mother in. They took her in. So now, they're taking us to the ghetto. We wanted to be together with my-- our mother. So who going to bring her home? So-- excuse me. So nobody's supposed to leave the house. And I had the problem-- we had payis-- you know, the payis-- who was always in my-- our way. Because right away, I was on the street. They recognized me that I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're not supposed to be on the street. I don't have to tell you what would happen. So I went into a little town, [NON-ENGLISH]. It's about 10 miles. And I hired the taxi. And I went with him to this town, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I ask the hospital to let my mother come into the taxi. And I brought my mother home just a day before they were taking us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we managed. We were happy that at least the whole family-- the whole family-- just the one who is there are together. And that's the way she ended up in Satoraljaujhely with the-- with the the wagon, on the Spiegel's wagon. So the rest of the Jews, everybody were walking 11 kilometers into the ghetto. What belongings did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just mainly covers. You couldn't-- how much can you carry with you? You put everything in a sheet, and you put it on your back and walking, because they didn't let you take furniture. We didn't know where they are taking us. We didn't know at all, what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in the ghetto, what happened? We had lots of people. They knew the family. So we went into-- there was some relatives, because every Jewish home had to take in-- they put in, in every room, maybe, six, seven, eight people, because there were no place, you know? They cut off so much for the city. And in every house, they just pushed in the Jews from around the neighborhood. Would you remember a street name that enclosed the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it was near the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you heard about [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Hungarian noble and a writer or a politician. And that was not far from the house where I was, because across the street, there was a Jewish school. And from this Jewish school-- I'm talking about not the Yeshiva, the school-- and high school and elementary school, the private school. That's where they made a temporary hospital. That's where they put my mother in, because my mother, she couldn't walk anymore. She was very, very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto enclosed in any way? A fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. They closed it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were fences, you know that made-- you know, and there were orders. You're not supposed to get across the street. Like, let's say, from my house, where I was staying with my parents, you know, with my father and my sister and brother, I couldn't go across the street to go to see my mother, because the Hungarian Gestapo, they were there. They were beating everybody. Anybody who was on street, they used to beat like I told you, with stuff like this, what I have there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarian Gendarmes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Gendarmes-- the Hungarian Gestapo, the Hungarian detectives.

speaker: interviewer
text: They guarded you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with food? Were there ration cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What happened-- they didn't give food. What we prepared ourselves, I remember, we used to make from milk, conserve milk, heavy. You know? And we used to eat with the spoon. We had a little bread we took with us still-- whatever we had, what doesn't get spoiled. So that's what we used to eat with milk, conserved milk. If you ever have it, it's like caramel-- that same thing, with bread, because they didn't give us food. I can show you who brought me food-- a neighbor's son, who was a Christian family. He was very nice with us. They used to bring me, on the gate. He used to throw over the food from Somotor. He used to come into school. So he used to bring us the food. So he pushed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a picture also there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mejarosz Gesa. Gesa, Mejarosz. The father was very, very religious. He used to love to come to discuss the Bible with my father. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people go out to work while they were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You couldn't leave. You couldn't do nothing. You had to stay. Nobody knew, what's going to happen. You were, approximately, I think, three weeks, two, three weeks, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? No, no, no radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know nothing. The only thing we knew, that the time came, the order came that everybody has to cut, now, the beard. And I was so annoyed with the payis because I had so much trouble with this payis. The anti-Semite youngsters, right away, they recognize me. And I ask my father, and I wouldn't dare tell them, please, let me cut it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: But finally, in the ghetto, I ask my father, Daddy, can I cut it off already now? Because he had to cut off his beard already, so he's giving me the OK, yes, because it was the order, already. We had to cut it off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How were orders given to you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pamphlets thrown on walls, and the loudspeakers. And there was, you know, messengers that came in to give. There was somebody who was between the Jews, some leaders, temporary leaders. They used the Jews to pass the messages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the way, everyday, you know, how did we find out that we have to go to the railroad. So they came. This part, they cut off. Let's see, 15, 20 houses-- everybody out on the street. And they take you to the railroad station. Were your grandparents still with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, my grandparents-- my grandfather died in '43, by natural, because he was an older man. And my grandmother, in [NON-ENGLISH], she died, also around 1940, maybe, something like that. I remember because I was studying there. So only my father, my mother, my sister, Zippora, who lives in [NON-ENGLISH], my brother Jacob was in the labor camp, already in Russia, you know? And my brother, David, also, we'd never heard about-- from him. And my sister, Toby-- she was eight or nine years old. And my brother Joseph Betzalel, and me, and my sister's little boy, maybe two-and-a-half, three years old. See, always, one child was in our place for a year to give a break for my sister, because she had three children. And she had to take care of the business. In the [INAUDIBLE], that's very, very, very hard for her. So in order to give her a break, that's what we used to go. And then we couldn't bring back the child anymore to my sister, because the Jew were not supposed to travel. So that's he way the child ended up with us. And that's the way they took the little boy, also with us, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day you were deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I can tell you by weeks. Approximately, I think, the 15 of April, approximately, they took us to the ghetto, right away, the next day after Passover. And I know, we were approximately two or three weeks in the ghetto, because we were the first transport. They took us to Auschwitz. So but I didn't know that we are-- where they're going to take us. We thought they are taking us somewhere, where-- because the Germans, they don't trust us, the Jews, because we are against the Germans, obviously-- so we thought they're going to take us somewhere to be away. You're not to be spies for the Allies. You know, that's what we thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was with you the day that you were deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day? My father, my sister, who lives in [NON-ENGLISH], Zippora, my sister, Toby, my brothers, Betzalel, whose name is Joseph, me, and the little nephew, my sister's little boy, was two-and-a-half or three, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was the last time you saw your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] [DRINKS] Before going to the train station-- we're not supposed to leave the house. So I ask my father, we have to tell my mother that we are leaving. If he would go out, they're going to kill him, the Gestapo. My sister-- you know, everybody's afraid. I was always-- somehow, I was not afraid. I look out through the window. And I don't see the Gestapo on the street. They were, I don't know, in the back, maybe 200 feet away or 200 yard. And I run out from the house, and I run across the street. And I run into the hospital. They didn't see me. So I'm in the hospital. So now I have to look for my mother. She's laying. There's no beds, nothing. They're laying on some kind of a mattress or something. And I find my mother there. And I bend down to her. I said, mother, they're taking us, already, away. I came to say goodbye. [CRYING] And she's telling me, I have no more children. [CRYING] [SOBBING] I can't-- every time I think about it, even when I drive the cab in New York, and I'm embarrassed. People are coming into the cab. And I break down. I start to cry. To say I have no more children. And I'm kissing mother. I have to go because they're going to beat me up. And then she couldn't say nothing else. And I just kiss her and hug her. I couldn't leave her [INAUDIBLE]. And I left her there. And I run across the street. I look if the Gestapo is there. And I see they're with their back this way. So when they were not looking, so I run across. It's only, like, from here to the house across the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I run back to the house where we were staying. And I was back. And I tell my father that I saw Mommy, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell him. Of course, we all start to cry. And a few minutes later, the Gestapo come.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Everybody out.' And they take-- we are walking. So walking where? We didn't even know where they're taking us. So we are walking. And so I know the area, because I studied there. They're taking us to the railroad station. There was two stations-- a big railroad station and a small one. So they take us there and into the freight train. They push us in. The luggage-- they didn't let you take nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just took, you know, to cover, and maybe a little bread, and the milk. This was very important, because this, we always-- you take a spoon. It kept you going. You didn't took too much place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, 'everybody in!' They start to chase us, hit, beating into the freight train, yeah? So we went in, about 80 or 90 people, you know, elderly women, elderly men, and children-- everyone into the same wagon, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, when it fill up, they close the door for the freight train. And after this, when everybody was in the train, there was a big, long building there. They used to make disinfection because they used to-- the front used to be in Russia, when the Hungarians and Germans-- they used to take the trains down to Russia. The train come back. And they put in the train empty to disinfect it. And it was a big gate to open, just enough to go in for the train. There was no windows, nothing, because that was the purpose, to kill the germs. And they pushed in the whole train to this. It was like a tunnel. And they closed the door even. There was no air. After 45 minutes, everybody gets panicking, yelling, there's no air! We are choking to death! Everybody get crazy-- heart attacks. And-- and we are yelling. Anybody who was near the door, sometimes we opened the door a little bit, to let in a little air through the door. And you hear the Germans are yelling, [SPEAKING GERMAN]! 'You dirty Jew!' in Hungarian. 'Drop dead!' And for hours, hours. Finally, maybe after three hours or four hours, when it was dark, the locomotive came. And they pulled out the train. Finally, we have a little air for the ones who was alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train is going where? I-- we had no idea where we are going. So we went. We traveled, maybe three, four days. So you measure-- my father, people like him, you had to relieve yourself. There is woman, children. There is no place, no toilet, nothing. Everyone using the little corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just-- you become an animal. You are dehumanized completely. So after this, we arrived in three, four days. I see Katowice. through the-- there was no window, just through the door there. I see Katowice. I knew it's in Poland. But I didn't know about Auschwitz. I didn't know nothing, what Auschwitz is. So we are going and going. All of a sudden, we arrive, Auschwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: What is Auschwitz? They opened the door. I looked-- SS with the German shepherds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yelling, with the sticks. And they're opening the freight train. And they chased, us. 'Raus!' Everybody was completely confused. Three, four days, locked in there, no food-- you were already dehumanized. You were like a zombie. You didn't know if you are alive or not. So we jumped down, the train, you know, down to the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm trying to keep the family together-- my father, my sister, my brother, then my little sister and my little nephew. I'm trying. But it didn't work like this, because the SS came.

speaker: interviewee
text: They start to beat the hell out of everybody. Hit over the head. Just 'Raus! Raus! Raus! Schnell! Schnell!'

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go. All of a sudden, you know, I'm losing everyone. My father is to the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother, Betzalel is to the left of me. And I see my little sister yelling to my father, 'Father! Wait for me!' But he couldn't because they were beating him up. And my little nephew, he was two-and-a-half years old is saying [INAUDIBLE] Wait for me!' I'm watching, the baby's crying. And they're beating the hell out of everybody. I said, this is-- I cannot take it. I'm dying! I don't know what's going on. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So in two minutes, I don't see my father. I don't see my sister. I don't see nobody. The only thing is I see-- I hold my hand for my brother, Joseph. I say, let's not get separated. Let's hold together, please. So when Father goes away, [? imagine, ?] he still had enough power in him to tell my brother, Joseph, Betzalel, please watch over Hershel, because I was the young one, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were frightened because he knew that I do things. You know, I'm more-- I'm not afraid. So he was older, my-- 2 and 1/2 years from me. So he-- somehow I listened to my brother like I would listen to my father. And we stick together in Auschwitz-- actually, Birkenau, I think. That's what it was-- a part of Auschwitz. So when we get there, in two minutes, you had to undress, you know. We didn't know what's going on. We're naked there. We stay in line, stay in line again, beating, get into the empty barrack. And finally, they make us go in, take a shower. And I see they're giving us clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured, if they're giving us clothing, that means we're going to go to work. Why would they give us, you know, those striped uniform? So they pushed us back in the barracks there for the night. There's no beds yet, only the concrete floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are already dead tired from four days of traveling, and no food, and nothing. We are hungry. And I have to sleep on the concrete. I cannot. You know, it's hard. And I have nothing to put under my head. But we stick there, with my brother. Let's see what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next day, or the two days later, finally, there were beds. You know, they were sleeping about 10 people in the beds. You know, those bunk beds, you know? And every half an hour, [NON-ENGLISH], you know? This [INAUDIBLE] selection, counting, selection. I didn't know, should I go first within the first one? Or should I be the last one? You know, you don't know what's going on. So we managed somehow to stay together with my brother. You know, and finally after, I think, two weeks, they take us out from there. And they put us in a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're going again, somewhere. They give us a half a bread, and I don't know, a little water. And we're going. I didn't know where we are going. So my brother, you know, he encourages me. You know, he was always-- he was the guy. He was a scholar, already then. He was such a nice kid-- Everything, just never complained, nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gives me, from his piece of bread. You know, I put the bread in my pocket. So I used to take a little piece. If I eat it up right away, I'm going to be hungry until next day. I mean, they didn't give us, in the train, nothing. just for the three days, just give us half a bread. It was, maybe, I don't know, a pound? So it was not bread. It was more sawdust, you know, than flour. So we were sitting in the train. If we yelled out-- on top of the freight train there are little windows. You know how it's built. We yell out, give us water! If you yelled out, they saw your face, they were shooting in through the window, because you were not supposed to see where you are going. So we arrive, finally. Three days, I think it took, from Auschwitz to go to Mauthausen. Excuse me. How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about two weeks, three weeks, because took us right away. They take the transport. And they'd send into different camps. So when we arrive in Mauthausen, finally the train stops. Dizzy, because you sleep on the floor. There's no place. They pushed in, again, about 90 people. There's no place even to sit. And they open the door. Again, the Germans, the SS, with the German shepherds and beating and beating.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to stay in lines. Right away, one, two, three, like in the army. What happened? This SS officer comes over to my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] He takes out the pistol and he beats the hell out of him, breaks his face and head, blood all over, I wish they would do it to me. And my heart was going oh, I loved him so much. And here, exactly, my brother, because he didn't go in the line too fast. So he took the pistol. He hit him, the nose, the forehead, the face. And he-- and he wouldn't complain. Finally, he took away his eyes from him. And thanks God, at least he's not-- you know, because when they are angry, they shoot you on the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once we are in the line, we start to walk. So I figure maybe he's going to leave us alone already. So no, start walking up to Mauthausen, from the railroad station.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is actually, here. You can see it here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the camp. Probably we'll see it later, showing the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get to Mauthausen. So the same thing-- everything goes by beating. You cannot walk, just running. And they hit you over the head. And go, run here. Run there. Take a shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: But finally, they started to give us a little food. So-- but before they put us into the barracks, they put-- they put us out. There was an empty place there in Mauthausen. And they start to make selections again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now i said to my brother, let's stay together. What happened-- they divided us. Like, you and I, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I get stuck on this side. And he gets stuck in the other side. And for a moment, I said, Betzalel, come over this side fast. He was afraid, the poor guy, because he was afraid that they're going to beat the hell out of him. And what happened, this was the last time I saw my brother. They took him away. I didn't know where. And I end up in Mauthausen. And from Mauthausen, after a few days, they took us to Gusen eins, Gusen 1. And then, they took us to Gusen 2. And I didn't see my brother. The only thing I know about my brother, that after the war, my cousin came home. And he told me that my brother died. The English were bombing the city. And the bomb fell on his barrack, where he was sleeping. And that's the way he died, in Melk. That's the way I found out that he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sub-camp of Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was-- yeah, it's a part of it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any registration in Auschwitz or Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, they gave me a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. But I don't understand why. I don't-- I don't have the tattoo. I will never understand. Somebody told me because I was too young. Apparently, I was supposed to go to the gas chamber. I don't know. I know that some others, they got the number, and I didn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, only in German. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Only in German I remember it. 67,917. Were you asked how old you were by anyone. Officers? Were you asked your age? Were you asked any questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they knew. I had to give-- because every block had the block schreiber, you know, the block writer, you know. Everything was there. My name, when I was born-- everybody had this in the [? data ?] office. So they knew when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you tell them your real age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, from Mauthausen to Gusen, how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. It's-- first, we went to Gusen 1. In Gusen 1, we stayed there temporary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe an hour. It's maybe, I don't know, four kilometers, or something like this. It's not so far, because I did it by car, you know, when I went to visit Mauthausen. It's not so far, but approximately an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still in the same uniform that was given from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give me-- what do they call those shoes, the wood, you know, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Clogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was like sneakers. But it was material, not leather. The sole was wood. This is like the Dutch, you know, they walk in Holland. They walk in those kind of shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked for an hour to Gusen 1?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, they put us in. They let us sleep. And actually, it was somehow more humane there. There was a bed and even the mattress had a little something in it, to make it a little bit softer. But we didn't work there. It was just we were waiting a week, or something like this without doing. And the only thing they did, every three or four times a day, appell. They constantly were counting, and counting, and the [INAUDIBLE], and selections. And that's what they were doing. After this, you know, a week later, they took us to Gusen 2. That was because the camp was not finished yet. They were building the barracks. And that's why they were keeping us in Mauthausen and Gusen 1, to get into Gusen 2. And when the barracks were finished, they put us in there. And I don't know how many hundred, because in one barrack, we were 600, 700. So it must be a couple thousand. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, we get into the barrack. They took the block six-- the number. There is a block [GERMAN]-- I don't know if you know what it means. He was the head of the block-- [GERMAN], block schreiber, and the one who took care of the kitchen, you know, for the food, to give out the food. But those are all prisoners, Germans. They were not from the German army. Germans who were in charge in the concentration camp-- they were doing the work, the orders, what they get from the SS outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the [GERMAN], he made a speech when. We get in there, to the barrack, we are to stay like this, in a circle. And he says-- in German, he was talking to us. Everybody understood German. If you don't behave, if you will do anything against the law here, you know what's going to happen to you? He does like this. He grabs one Jew. He puts him down to the floor. He takes a 2x4. He puts it on his neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's jumping on his neck. Everybody had to watch the way he killed. He was a lawyer, Dr. Tziner. His-- his brother was the doctor for my mother, Dr. Tziner. One was a lawyer. The other one was a doctor. And then, when he was dead, he grabs another Jew. He could've been me. If I would be near his hand, it would be me. He grabbed another one. He puts him down on the floor. He put the 2x4 on his neck. He jumps. Then he grabs a third Jew. He says, you're going to be the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't know if you know what [NON-ENGLISH] means-- the murderer.
